text:
{
 "prose": "At dawn Maren sends the ship away without her, standing on the landing until its sails are a gray smudge and then nothing at all, and climbs back up to trim the wick the way she has every morning of her keeping.

She takes the ledger down from its shelf that evening, unwraps the oilcloth, and finds the blank line waiting where it has always waited. She writes her name there, in a hand steadier than she expected, and the bargain that has run three generations deep closes itself around her like a held breath finally let out.

The sea settles in the days after the way a dog settles by a fire, all its restlessness folded down into something that could almost pass, from a fishing boat or a passing packet, for an ordinary rock with an ordinary light on it. The coast never learns what it was spared. That, Maren comes to understand, watching the wick every night at the hours it demands, is the exact shape of the price: not pain, not loss anyone else would recognize, but a silence nobody on the mainland will ever think to be grateful for.

The coast sleeps because Maren does not. She keeps the long watch her grandmother kept, and her grandmother's mother before that, trimming the lamp at hours no clock quite agrees with, feeling the tide work at the stilts below her floor.

Some nights, though, a corrected chart arrives by the weekly post, addressed in a tidy, familiar hand, showing water exactly as deep as it ought to be, and on those nights the watch feels a little less like keeping, and a little more like being kept.
",
 "micro_details": []
}
